text.title: CSS-in-JS Advanced Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the CSS-in-JS Advanced methodology. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; the last rule binds the decision tree skip path.

rule.statement: SSR pipeline MUST extract critical CSS to the HTML head. Hydration FOUC is rejected.
rule.rationale: un-extracted critical CSS adds 200ms+ to LCP.

rule.statement: Library choice MUST document its runtime cost (e.g. styled-components ~12kB gzipped; Linaria ~0kB). Choices without budget are rejected.
rule.rationale: Performance budgets are decisions, not aspirations.

rule.statement: Spec declares atomic-CSS strategy (Tailwind-like, Panda) vs monolithic (styled-components style strings). Mixing both inside one component library is rejected.
Theme switch strategy (CSS variables runtime OR build-time variants) MUST be declared; the choice constrains library options.
rule.rationale: Different libs constrain different theming approaches; mismatched picks lead to rewrites.

rule.statement: Spec MUST evaluate at least one zero-runtime alternative (Linaria, vanilla-extract, Panda) before committing to a runtime library.
rule.rationale: runtime CSS-in-JS is shrinking in 2025+; un-evaluated commits become migrations.

rule.statement: If the framework supports React Server Components (Next.js App Router), library MUST be RSC-compatible OR the spec documents the rendering-boundary workaround.
rule.rationale: RSC + runtime CSS-in-JS combinations are well-known footguns.

rule.statement: When the decision tree routes to `skip-this-methodology`, the agent MUST emit a structured `skip` decision (with reason + suggested-sibling) rather than running CSS-in-JS Advanced under degraded conditions.
rule.rationale: decision-tree contract. The decision tree is the only place that knows the routing; skipping must be observable so the orchestrator can log the routed-elsewhere path.
